verse_specific_anchors: tūṣṇīṁ babhūva ha — Arjuna fell silent (with the emphatic 'ha'); the engineering analog explicitly reads this as a marker of case-closing, not as a teaching
na yotsya iti — 'I will not fight'; the analog renders this as the engineering director's 'I am not going to lead this migration'
the verse-as-scaffolding role: the explicit chapter-architecture point that this verse is the marker between Arjuna's case and Krishna's reply
verdict_reason: I tested against three candidate verses where 'going silent' might be claimed: BG 2.30 (Krishna says one need not grieve for any being), BG 6.7 (the conqueror-of-self is settled), BG 11.49 (Arjuna trembles after the vision). None of these turn on a silence-marking-end-of-case. The engineering analog is anchored specifically on the structural-pivot reading — 'the verse marks the moment that Arjuna's case has closed and the teaching is about to open.' This is verse-2.9-specific scaffolding. The analog also explicitly disqualifies the generic 'silence is wisdom' reading via its counter-example. PASS — the analog could not apply to a non-narrative-pivot verse.
